question: [File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with deadline == block.timestamp - 1 (one second in the past) and observe whether the Expired() check correctly rejects it, or whether timestamp manipulation within the same block allows it to pass under PRECONDITIONS: attacker sets deadline to block.timestamp - 1 at submission time, CALL_SEQUENCE: setIsAuthorized(auth_with_past_deadline, sig), violating INVARIANT: block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline must strictly reject past deadlines, causing scoped impact: confirm expired authorization is correctly rejected — if not, stale signatures could be replayed? Proof idea: unit test with deadline = block.timestamp - 1, assert Expired() reverts.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized delegate of the victim use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to the EcrecoverRatifier contract, enabling the ratifier to act as a full Midnight delegate for the victim and sign offer fills on their behalf under PRECONDITIONS: victim authorizes attacker, attacker signs Authorization{authorized=ecrecoverRatifier, isAuthorized=true}, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth_granting_ratifier, attacker_sig), (2) EcrecoverRatifier is now authorized for victim, (3) any offer signed by EcrecoverRatifier (which cannot sign) passes isAuthorized check, violating INVARIANT: ratifier authorization must not grant the ratifier contract itself delegation over user accounts, causing scoped impact: ratifier privilege escalation — EcrecoverRatifier gains full account delegation? Proof idea: grant ratifier as authorized, assert isAuthorized[victim][ecrecoverRatifier] is true and trace downstream impact.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: IdLib.toId] Can an attacker construct two distinct Market structs that produce the same toId output (hash collision) by exploiting abi.encodePacked ambiguity in the inner keccak256(abi.encodePacked(SSTORE2_PREFIX, abi.encode(market))) under PRECONDITIONS: attacker finds two Market structs M1, M2 where abi.encode(M1) != abi.encode(M2) but keccak256(abi.encodePacked(SSTORE2_PREFIX, abi.encode(M1))) == keccak256(abi.encodePacked(SSTORE2_PREFIX, abi.encode(M2))), CALL_SEQUENCE: touchMarket(M1), touchMarket(M2), observe same id, violating INVARIANT: toId must be injective over all valid Market structs, causing scoped impact: market ID collision — two markets share accounting state, enabling credit/debt confusion? Proof idea: fuzz Market structs with varying collateralParams lengths, assert toId injectivity.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where the AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH constant (0x81d0284f...) does not match keccak256('Authorization(address authorizer,address authorized,bool isAuthorized,uint256 nonce,uint256 deadline)') due to a typo or encoding error, causing all signatures to be computed against a wrong typehash under PRECONDITIONS: AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH is hardcoded, CALL_SEQUENCE: verify keccak256(AUTHORIZATION_TYPE) == AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH in a differential test, violating INVARIANT: hardcoded typehash must exactly match the string encoding of the struct type, causing scoped impact: all authorization signatures are invalid or accept wrong-typed data if typehash is wrong? Proof idea: differential test asserting keccak256('Authorization(address authorizer,address authorized,bool isAuthorized,uint256 nonce,uint256 deadline)') == AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer must itself be authorized in Midnight (isAuthorized[victim][ecrecoverAuthorizer]==true) to call setIsAuthorized on behalf of victim, and that this authorization is coarse-grained, meaning EcrecoverAuthorizer can also call withdraw, withdrawCollateral, take, repay, setConsumed on behalf of victim under PRECONDITIONS: victim calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, victim) to enable sig-based auth, CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls midnight.take(..., taker=victim, ...) where msg.sender=ecrecoverAuthorizer — but ecrecoverAuthorizer has no such function; confirm whether any path allows EcrecoverAuthorizer's authorization to be abused, violating INVARIANT: EcrecoverAuthorizer's Midnight authorization must only be exercisable through its own setIsAuthorized function, causing scoped impact: coarse authorization abuse — EcrecoverAuthorizer's delegation enables unintended Midnight actions? Proof idea: trace all Midnight functions that check isAuthorized[victim][ecrecoverAuthorizer], assert none are callable by arbitrary callers through EcrecoverAuthorizer.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker submit a ratifierData with an empty Merkle proof (proof.length == 0) and leafIndex == 0, causing isLeaf to return true when root == hashOffer(offer), effectively making any offer self-certifying without a real Merkle tree under PRECONDITIONS: attacker constructs offer O, computes root = HashLib.hashOffer(O), signs root, CALL_SEQUENCE: ecrecoverRatifier.isRatified(O, abi.encode(sig, root=hashOffer(O), 0, [])), violating INVARIANT: a Merkle proof with empty proof must only be valid for a single-leaf tree where root == leaf, causing scoped impact: confirm whether single-leaf self-certification is intended or allows unintended offer fills? Proof idea: submit empty proof with root=hashOffer(offer), assert isLeaf returns true and isRatified succeeds.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker use a valid signature for a Merkle root on EcrecoverRatifier instance A to fill offers on EcrecoverRatifier instance B (different address, same MIDNIGHT) by replaying the signature, since the domain separator includes address(this) but the root cancellation state is per-instance under PRECONDITIONS: maker signed root R for ratifier instance A, instance B is deployed with same MIDNIGHT, CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls midnight.take with offer.ratifier=instance_B and ratifierData containing sig for instance_A, violating INVARIANT: a ratifier signature must bind to the specific ratifier contract address, causing scoped impact: cross-ratifier signature replay — maker's signature fills offers through an unintended ratifier instance? Proof idea: deploy two EcrecoverRatifier instances, replay sig from A on B, assert domain separator mismatch prevents acceptance.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.liquidate] Can an attacker use a signed Authorization to grant themselves delegation over a victim-borrower, then call liquidate on the victim's own position (which requires the liquidator to be authorized by the liquidatorGate, not the borrower), under PRECONDITIONS: market has liquidatorGate, attacker holds signed Authorization from victim, victim is unhealthy, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) setIsAuthorized(grant, sig), (2) midnight.liquidate(market, ..., borrower=victim, ...) called by attacker, violating INVARIANT: liquidation authorization is controlled by liquidatorGate, not by borrower's isAuthorized mapping, causing scoped impact: confirm that borrower's isAuthorized delegation does not affect liquidatorGate checks — delegation must not enable or block liquidation? Proof idea: grant delegation, attempt liquidation, assert liquidatorGate check is independent of isAuthorized[victim][attacker].

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with a valid signature but where authorization.nonce is set to a value far in the future (e.g., nonce=1000) while the current nonce is 0, causing the InvalidNonce check to reject it, but also observe whether the nonce is still incremented before the check fires under PRECONDITIONS: current nonce[victim] == 0, attacker submits auth with nonce=1000, CALL_SEQUENCE: setIsAuthorized(auth_nonce_1000, sig), violating INVARIANT: nonce must not be incremented if the submitted nonce does not match the current nonce, causing scoped impact: confirm nonce is not burned on future-nonce submission — if it is, victim's nonce jumps to 1 unexpectedly? Proof idea: submit auth with nonce=1000, assert nonce[victim] remains 0 after InvalidNonce revert.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that abi.encode(authorization) in hashStruct encodes the Authorization struct including the bool isAuthorized field, and verify whether bool encoding in abi.encode pads to 32 bytes, potentially causing a typehash mismatch if a wallet signs using a different bool encoding under PRECONDITIONS: victim's wallet encodes bool as 1 byte, EcrecoverAuthorizer uses abi.encode which pads bool to 32 bytes, CALL_SEQUENCE: victim signs with non-standard bool encoding, attacker submits, violating INVARIANT: the digest computed on-chain must exactly match the digest the signer computed off-chain, causing scoped impact: signature rejection or acceptance of wrong-encoded authorization — if encoding differs, valid sigs are rejected or invalid sigs accepted? Proof idea: differential test comparing abi.encode(Authorization) bool encoding against EIP-712 standard bool encoding.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.withdraw] Can an attacker use a signed Authorization to grant themselves delegation, call withdraw to move a victim-lender's credit to the attacker's address, then revoke the delegation, under PRECONDITIONS: victim-lender has credit in a market, attacker holds signed Authorization{authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true}, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) setIsAuthorized(grant, sig), (2) midnight.withdraw(market, units, onBehalf=victim, receiver=attacker), (3) setIsAuthorized(revoke, sig2), violating INVARIANT: credit withdrawal must only be authorized by the credit holder or their explicitly trusted delegate, causing scoped impact: direct credit theft — attacker drains victim's lender credit to their own address? Proof idea: grant→withdraw→revoke, assert loanToken.balanceOf(attacker) increases by victim's credit amount.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where the EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH constant (0x47e79534...) does not match keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)') due to a hardcoding error, causing the domain separator to be computed incorrectly and all signatures to be accepted or rejected incorrectly under PRECONDITIONS: EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH is hardcoded in IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol, CALL_SEQUENCE: differential test asserting keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)') == EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH, violating INVARIANT: hardcoded domain typehash must exactly match the string encoding, causing scoped impact: all authorization signatures computed with wrong domain separator — either all valid sigs rejected or wrong-domain sigs accepted? Proof idea: assertEq(keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)'), EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH).

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash] Can an attacker submit a ratifierData with proof.length > 20 to trigger the TreeTooHigh revert in offerTreeTypeHash, causing a DoS on any take that uses a deep Merkle tree under PRECONDITIONS: maker signed a root for a tree of height > 20, taker submits ratifierData with proof.length=21, CALL_SEQUENCE: midnight.take(offer, ratifierData_with_21_proof_elements, ...), violating INVARIANT: valid offers in trees of height <= 20 must always be fillable, causing scoped impact: offer fill DoS — taker cannot fill a valid offer if the tree height exceeds 20? Proof idea: construct proof with length=21, assert TreeTooHigh reverts in isRatified.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who observes a victim's signed Authorization in the mempool modify the signature's v component (27 vs 28) to produce a different but potentially valid ecrecover output, enabling them to submit a modified signature that recovers to a different address under PRECONDITIONS: victim broadcasts signed Authorization, attacker flips v from 27 to 28, CALL_SEQUENCE: setIsAuthorized(auth, Signature{v=flipped, r=original, s=original}), violating INVARIANT: signature malleability must not allow a different signer address to be recovered from a modified signature, causing scoped impact: signature malleability — attacker submits modified sig that recovers to a different address, potentially one that is authorized? Proof idea: flip v component, assert ecrecover returns different address, assert Unauthorized() fires if that address is not authorized.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.supplyCollateral] Can an attacker use a signed Authorization to grant themselves delegation over a victim, call supplyCollateral to deposit attacker-controlled collateral into the victim's position (activating a collateral slot), then revoke delegation, leaving the victim with an activated collateral they cannot remove without oracle interaction under PRECONDITIONS: victim has no collateral, attacker holds signed Authorization, attacker has collateral tokens, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) grant delegation, (2) supplyCollateral(market, 0, amount, victim), (3) revoke delegation, violating INVARIANT: collateral activation must only occur with the account owner's consent, causing scoped impact: collateral poisoning — victim's position has an activated collateral with a potentially reverting oracle, blocking future liquidation or health checks? Proof idea: grant→supplyCollateral→revoke, assert victim's collateralBitmap has unexpected bit set.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorized == authorization.authorizer == msg.sender (attacker), and the attacker is already authorized by the victim in Midnight, causing the signer check (signer == authorizer || isAuthorized(authorizer, signer)) to pass with signer=attacker and authorizer=victim, granting the attacker a new authorization entry under PRECONDITIONS: victim has authorized attacker in Midnight, attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=current}, CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsAuthorized(auth, attacker_sig), violating INVARIANT: an authorized delegate must not be able to use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant themselves additional Midnight authorizations beyond what the authorizer directly granted, causing scoped impact: delegation self-reinforcement — attacker uses their existing delegation to create a new EcrecoverAuthorizer-mediated delegation entry? Proof idea: authorized delegate signs auth for themselves, assert isAuthorized[victim][attacker] is set via EcrecoverAuthorizer path.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: IdLib.toId / Midnight.toId] Can an attacker observe that Midnight.toId is a view function (not pure) because it reads INITIAL_CHAIN_ID from immutable storage, and that calling toId with a market struct containing dynamic arrays (collateralParams) via abi.encodePacked may produce different results depending on ABI encoding of the dynamic array, causing market ID instability under PRECONDITIONS: market has multiple collateralParams, attacker calls toId with same market struct encoded differently, CALL_SEQUENCE: toId(market_with_n_collaterals) vs toId(market_with_same_collaterals_different_encoding), violating INVARIANT: toId must return the same bytes32 for semantically identical markets regardless of how the struct is passed, causing scoped impact: market ID instability — same market resolves to different IDs depending on callsite encoding? Proof idea: fuzz Market struct encoding, assert toId is deterministic for identical semantic content.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with a valid signature but where the deadline is set to exactly block.timestamp of a future block, sign it, and then have a validator delay the transaction until after that block, causing the authorization to be submitted at exactly the deadline boundary and accepted under PRECONDITIONS: victim signs Authorization with deadline=T, transaction is delayed until block.timestamp==T, CALL_SEQUENCE: setIsAuthorized submitted at block.timestamp==T, check block.timestamp <= deadline passes, violating INVARIANT: deadline-boundary authorizations must be consistently accepted or rejected, causing scoped impact: confirm deadline boundary behavior is intentional — block.timestamp == deadline is accepted by design? Proof idea: vm.warp(deadline), call setIsAuthorized, assert it succeeds.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / HashLib.hashOffer] Can an attacker construct two different Offer structs that produce the same hashOffer output by exploiting the keccak256(offer.callbackData) encoding of the dynamic bytes field, enabling them to substitute one offer for another in a Merkle tree under PRECONDITIONS: attacker finds O1, O2 where all fixed fields are equal but callbackData differs yet keccak256(O1.callbackData) == keccak256(O2.callbackData) (hash collision), CALL_SEQUENCE: maker signs tree containing O1, attacker fills with O2, violating INVARIANT: hashOffer must be collision-resistant over all Offer fields including callbackData, causing scoped impact: offer substitution — attacker fills an offer with different callbackData than the maker intended, potentially triggering unintended callback behavior? Proof idea: fuzz callbackData pairs, assert hashOffer collision resistance.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who holds a signed Authorization for isAuthorized=true submit it, then immediately in the same block submit another Authorization for isAuthorized=false (with nonce+1, also pre-signed by victim), effectively granting and revoking in the same block, but observe whether any intermediate state (between the two txs in the same block) can be exploited by a third party under PRECONDITIONS: victim pre-signs both grant(nonce=0) and revoke(nonce=1), attacker submits both in same block, CALL_SEQUENCE: tx1: setIsAuthorized(grant, sig0), tx2: setIsAuthorized(revoke, sig1), violating INVARIANT: intermediate authorization state within a block must not be exploitable by front-running between the two transactions, causing scoped impact: transient delegation window — a third party observing the mempool exploits the grant before the revoke lands? Proof idea: submit grant and revoke in same block, assert no third-party can exploit the window between them.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.repay] Can an attacker use a signed Authorization to grant themselves delegation over a victim-borrower, call repay on behalf of the victim using the attacker's own funds (increasing victim's withdrawable credit), then immediately withdraw that credit to the attacker's address under PRECONDITIONS: victim has debt, attacker holds signed Authorization and loan tokens, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) grant delegation, (2) midnight.repay(market, units, onBehalf=victim, callback=0, ''), (3) midnight.withdraw(market, units, onBehalf=victim, receiver=attacker), violating INVARIANT: repayment credit must accrue to the borrower and only be withdrawable by the borrower or their authorized delegate, causing scoped impact: credit redirection — attacker repays victim's debt and immediately withdraws the resulting credit to themselves? Proof idea: grant→repay→withdraw sequence, assert attacker receives loan tokens equal to repaid amount.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker fuzz all fields of the Authorization struct (authorizer, authorized, isAuthorized, nonce, deadline) and all fields of the Signature struct (v, r, s) to find any combination where ecrecover returns a non-zero address that satisfies the isAuthorized check without the authorizer's knowledge, under PRECONDITIONS: no prior authorization exists, CALL_SEQUENCE: fuzz(Authorization, Signature) → setIsAuthorized, violating INVARIANT: no unprivileged attacker should be able to pass the signer check without a valid signature from the authorizer or their delegate, causing scoped impact: signature forgery — attacker grants themselves delegation without a valid signature? Proof idea: stateful fuzz test over all Authorization and Signature fields, assert Unauthorized() always fires for non-authorizer signers.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot] Can an attacker who is authorized by a maker cancel a root, then have the maker re-sign the same root (same offers), and observe whether the canceled root can be re-used after the maker re-signs it, since isRootCanceled is permanent and cannot be un-canceled under PRECONDITIONS: maker authorizes attacker, attacker cancels root R, maker re-signs root R, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) cancelRoot(maker, R), (2) maker re-signs R, (3) taker submits take with ratifierData for R, violating INVARIANT: a canceled root must remain permanently invalid regardless of re-signing, causing scoped impact: permanent offer tree invalidation — maker cannot reuse a canceled root even with fresh signatures? Proof idea: cancel root, re-sign same root, assert isRatified reverts with RootCanceled.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.multicall] Can an attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically: (1) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized (external call, not part of multicall), then (2) within the same multicall, call withdraw on behalf of victim, exploiting the fact that multicall uses delegatecall and the authorization state is already set from the external call under PRECONDITIONS: attacker pre-submits setIsAuthorized externally, then calls multicall([withdraw_on_behalf_of_victim]), CALL_SEQUENCE: external setIsAuthorized → multicall([withdraw]), violating INVARIANT: multicall must not allow exploitation of authorization state set in the same transaction by an external call, causing scoped impact: authorization state exploitation via multicall — attacker drains victim's credit in same transaction as authorization grant? Proof idea: sequence external setIsAuthorized + multicall withdraw in same transaction, assert victim's credit decreases.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: IdLib.toId / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker observe that HashLib.hashMarket (used in hashOffer for EcrecoverRatifier) and IdLib.toId (used for market IDs) use different encoding schemes for the same Market struct — hashMarket uses EIP-712 recursive hashing while toId uses abi.encodePacked+abi.encode — and exploit this divergence to construct a market that hashes differently in each context, causing an offer to be ratified for a market that does not correspond to the actual market ID under PRECONDITIONS: attacker constructs Market M where hashMarket(M) matches a different market's EIP-712 hash, CALL_SEQUENCE: take(offer_with_M, ratifierData), observe ratifier accepts offer for market M but Midnight routes to toId(M), violating INVARIANT: the market referenced in a ratified offer must correspond to the actual market ID used for accounting, causing scoped impact: market identity confusion — ratifier approves offer for a different market than the one being traded? Proof idea: differential test comparing hashMarket vs toId encoding for same Market struct.
